message:
fix(cli): require .write.lock for `br sync --flush-only` to prevent fsqlite concurrent-write deadlock

`needs_write_lock` in `main.rs` treated `br sync --flush-only` as a
read-only mode and skipped acquiring the process-wide `.beads/.write.lock`.
That was wrong: `--flush-only` calls `finalize_export` inside a
`with_write_transaction`, which:

  - clears every dirty-issue marker that made it into the new export,
  - updates `metadata.jsonl_content_hash` / `last_export_time` /
    `needs_flush`,
  - writes the export-content hash row,

i.e. it is a write-side operation as far as fsqlite is concerned. Two
concurrent `br sync --flush-only` invocations — or one racing a
mutating command's auto-flush — could therefore hit the exact fsqlite
concurrent-write deadlock that `.write.lock` was introduced
(issue #243) to serialize.

The fix narrows the "skip write lock" carve-out to `--status`, which
is the only truly read-only sync subcommand. `--flush-only`,
`--merge`, `--import-only`, and the default sync path all now take
the lock. The inline comment pins the reasoning so a future reader
looking at `needs_write_lock(Commands::Sync(args))` sees why
`--flush-only` can't opt out.

Regression test: `sync_flush_only_requires_write_lock` parses each
sync-subcommand invocation through `Cli::parse_from` and asserts the
table of `needs_write_lock` outcomes:

  - `--flush-only` → true (the bug)
  - `--status`     → false (still the only read-only path)
  - `--merge` / `--import-only` / bare `sync` → true (unchanged)

This stays lightweight (no storage, no filesystem, just CLI parsing
and the `const fn`) so it runs in microseconds and will catch any
future regression that reintroduces the carve-out.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
